title: Signal + Judgment Trader
subtitle: An algorithmic trading system whose key finding is an honest negative result
description:
An algorithmic trading system for liquid US equities, built as a three-layer decision pipeline (ML signal model, LLM judgment filter, hard-coded risk gate) on top of an event-driven backtester I wrote first as the source of truth. The headline result is the one most trading projects bury:

| Metric | Gate | In-sample (lookahead, invalid) | **Walk-forward out-of-sample** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sharpe | ≥ 1.0 | 1.18 | **−0.02** |
| Max drawdown | ≤ 15% | 3.1% | **16.0%** |
| Profit factor | ≥ 1.3 | 1.70 | **0.99** |
| Net return | n/a | +14.6% | **−1.2%** |

The model looks excellent scored over its own training window and has no real edge out-of-sample. The score-to-return decile curve is flat to inverted (top-minus-bottom spread −0.03%), mean OOS AUC is 0.516 across 12 walk-forward folds with 3 folds below 0.5, and the losses sit almost entirely in the 2022 bear market. That is the textbook failure of long-only mean reversion: buying dips that keep dipping.

That gap is the actual finding. The system was built to catch this outcome before shipping it, not to hide it. The success gate was fixed in advance (Sharpe ≥ 1.0, max drawdown ≤ 15%, profit factor ≥ 1.3 after costs), validation ran walk-forward with an embargo, anti-lookahead was mutation-tested, and the design doc set an honest checkpoint: if out-of-sample results fail the gate, stop, do not loosen the gate or tweak the backtester. When the checkpoint fired I rejected the thesis on the evidence instead of bending the rules. "No real edge exists" was listed as the most likely risk on day one, so a system that knows when to stop is the point.

The engineering around that result is fully built and tested: a Pydantic-typed pipeline (signal, judgment, risk, execution) where each stage can only shrink or block what the stage before it proposed, a fail-closed Claude judgment agent, an immutable risk layer with mandatory stops and a kill switch, reproducible backtests keyed by config hash, and paper trading through Alpaca.

technicalChallenges.title: Walk-forward validation that can't fool you
technicalChallenges.description: The in-sample row shows the trap: naive evaluation gives Sharpe 1.18 purely from lookahead. To avoid it the system uses rolling walk-forward validation, with 4-year train windows, 6-month validation windows that tile history without overlap, and a 5-day embargo gap between train and validation equal to the label horizon, so no forward-return label leaks across the boundary. In the backtest each day is scored only by the fold whose validation window covers that day, so no prediction comes from a model that trained on its own date.

technicalChallenges.title: An LLM as a fail-closed trade filter, not a trader
technicalChallenges.description: The judgment layer sends each signal plus context (earnings calendar, macro events, news) to a Claude agent that returns APPROVE, REJECT, or REDUCE_SIZE with reasoning. The contract only runs one way: Claude can remove or shrink a trade but never create, enlarge, or redirect one, and that limit lives in code (Pydantic validators that clamp the size multiplier, a parser that accepts only the three decisions), not just in the prompt. Any failure such as a timeout, unparseable JSON, or schema violation resolves to REJECT, so an unreachable judge means no trade rather than a default approval.

technicalChallenges.title: Proving there is no lookahead, not just claiming it
technicalChallenges.description: Anti-lookahead is enforced with a poisoned-future test: fixtures corrupt every bar after a date D and assert that D's computed features are bit-identical, so any accidental use of future data breaks the suite straight away. Entry fills are modeled at the next day's open, matching the live fill convention, never the signal day's close, and every backtest result carries a config hash, so a run that can't be reproduced exactly does not count.

technicalChallenges.title: A checkpoint designed to kill the project
technicalChallenges.description: The design doc set the success gate and an honest checkpoint up front: if out-of-sample results don't approach the gate after reasonable iteration, stop, and don't loosen the gate, change the backtester, or re-tune on the same window. Diagnostics (decile calibration, score distribution, per-year PnL) showed the failure was structural, with no score-to-return relationship at all, so more feature tuning would only have manufactured overfitting. The checkpoint fired as planned, and the negative result was written up with full reproduction steps.

assets.imageAlt: Signal + Judgment Trader — algorithmic trading system architecture diagram

title: Automated Content Continuity Pipeline
subtitle: Async LLM pipeline detecting continuity conflicts across versioned documents
description: Designed and built a production-grade async backend that uses LLM-powered semantic analysis to detect continuity conflicts across versioned documents. Architected a fully decoupled pipeline with FastAPI, Celery/Redis for distributed task processing, and ChromaDB for high-dimensional vector search. Implemented JWT authentication, SHA-256 document deduplication, multi-format text extraction (PDF, DOCX, Markdown), and full schema migration management with Alembic. Integrated Gemini 2.0 Flash as the semantic reasoning layer for conflict detection across document versions.

technicalChallenges.title: Non-Blocking Async Document Chunking
technicalChallenges.description: Processing large multi-format documents synchronously would block FastAPI's async event loop, causing request timeouts under concurrent load. The solution offloads all extraction and chunking work to Celery workers via Redis as the message broker, keeping the main API process entirely non-blocking. Task status is tracked via a polling endpoint backed by database state, with no long-polling or WebSocket overhead on the server side.

technicalChallenges.title: Vector Embedding Search Latency in ChromaDB
technicalChallenges.description: Naïve cosine similarity scans across large document chunk collections produced query latencies exceeding acceptable thresholds as the corpus grew. Mitigated by tuning ChromaDB's HNSW index parameters (ef_construction, M) to balance recall accuracy against query time, and pre-filtering candidate chunks by document metadata before executing the embedding distance search. This reduced P95 query latency significantly while maintaining semantic retrieval precision above 90%.

technicalChallenges.title: SHA-256 Deduplication Across Multi-Format Inputs
technicalChallenges.description: Different document formats produce structurally identical content that naive byte-hash comparison treats as distinct, causing redundant processing and inflated vector storage. A normalised text extraction layer strips format-specific encoding before hashing, so semantically identical documents across PDF, DOCX, and Markdown representations hash identically and are deduplicated at ingestion time before any LLM call is dispatched.

assets.imageAlt: Architecture diagram of the Automated Content Continuity Pipeline showing FastAPI, Celery workers, Redis broker, ChromaDB vector store, and PostgreSQL

title: Dual-Design Portfolio Website
subtitle: Two fully distinct portfolio views switchable via a persistent toggle
description: A portfolio site with two completely separate views, a Professional Executive view and a PS Vita XMB-inspired view, switched by one persistent toggle. State runs on a small custom system built from localStorage and custom DOM events, with no framework. It stops the flash of the wrong view on load with a synchronous inline script that runs before the CSS is parsed, keeps the two views from interfering through strict `.pro-` and `.vita-` class prefixes, and plays UI sounds through a Howler.js audio sprite. Tuned for Core Web Vitals, with LCP under 1.2s and CLS at 0.00.

technicalChallenges.title: Stopping the flash of the wrong view on load
technicalChallenges.description: If either view renders before the saved preference is applied, the page flashes the wrong one on load. The fix is a tiny synchronous script placed as the first element in <head>, before any <link> or <style>, that reads localStorage and sets [data-view] on <html> before the CSS is parsed. Defer it or load it async and the flash comes back, so its position is a hard rule, not a preference.

technicalChallenges.title: Keeping two views from leaking styles into each other
technicalChallenges.description: Both views sit in the DOM at the same time, with CSS visibility deciding which one shows. Without strict naming, Professional styles bleed into Vita elements and the other way around, which corrupts the layout on toggle. Every selector is prefixed with .pro- or .vita-, every custom property is scoped under a [data-view] selector, and @layer ordering stops any utility rule from quietly overriding a view's component styles.

technicalChallenges.title: A sub-400ms view transition that still works everywhere
technicalChallenges.description: The View Transition API gives GPU-composited cross-fades in Chrome 111+, but older browsers need a real fallback. The transition manager checks for the API at runtime and picks the right path: either document.startViewTransition with custom ::view-transition-* keyframes, or a manual opacity and scale sequence driven by requestAnimationFrame. Both finish in under 400ms of perceived time.

assets.imageAlt: Side-by-side comparison of the Professional Executive View and PS Vita Immersive View of the portfolio
